---
title: "Lightweight tools for sparse single-cell data analysis"
author: ypp
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
tags:
- lightweight
- single-cell
- integrative analysis
---



TL;DR. We develop scalable and memory-efficient tools for single-cell data analysis.

---


{{< figure library="true" src="/project/mmutil_annotate.png" title="test" >}}


![](project/mmutil_annotate.png)

![](mmutil_annotate.png)


{{< figure library="true" src="project/cancer_genomics_research.png" title="Integrative cancer research builds on two principal axes of genomics data. **(A)** Population-level studies investigate variation across large cohorts. **(B)** Regulatory genomics researches delve into multiple layers of molecular networks." >}}








### There are already many tools.

Ever since single-cell technology was introduced to the world, every year, every month, we see more tools. See [scRNA-tools](https://www.scrna-tools.org/), for example. Single-cell data analysis often involves many steps of data processing, quantification, and statistical inference. The technology is imperfect, and each step deserves careful consideration of different types of approaches. We, bioinformaticians, test a different combination of small tools and tailor a pipeline that works best for each project. Perhaps, there is no master algorithm, and the competitions among computational biologists are indeed fierce.

The history of genomics repeats itself. We are probably seeing what had happened in RNA-seq, even gene expression micorarray analysis. Once we knew how to normalize scRNA-seq data, we would want to know how to test differentially-expressed genes. Then, we would want to build more audacious models, such as gene regulatory networks.  And we know there has been always a enough room for innovation and creativity.

Okay, that sounds meek and quite passive. Why do we dare to jump into the Wild, Wild, West?

[Eleven grand challenges in single-cell data science](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-020-1926-6)



### The underlying data matrix is sparse, really sparse.


no figure?

### A scalable approach to index millions of cells for a fast random access

Modifying the indexing scheme of [TABIX](http://www.htslib.org/doc/tabix.html), 
we 


### Test

test

<{{< figure library="true" src="project/mmutil_annotate.png" title="test" >}}


{{< figure library="true" src="project/cancer_genomics_research.png" title="Integrative cancer research builds on two principal axes of genomics data. **(A)** Population-level studies investigate variation across large cohorts. **(B)** Regulatory genomics researches delve into multiple layers of molecular networks." >}}
